---
title: "Best practices in R"
subtitle: "Course: Introduction to programming and data analysis in R"
author:
  - name: "Filip Andras & Efrain Garcia Sanchez"
    affiliations:
      - name: "CIMCYC — University of Granada"
date: "2026-04-16"
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 2
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
    page-layout: full
    include-in-header:
      - text: '<style>body { font-size: 14.5px; } pre { white-space: pre-wrap; word-wrap: break-word; font-size: 13px; } code { font-size: 0.9em; } .quarto-title-block, #quarto-content { max-width: 1100px; margin: auto; } #TOC { max-width: 220px; font-size: 13px; }</style>'
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Why best practices?

Imagine returning to an analysis **6 months later**, or a reviewer asking you to modify a figure, or a collaborator wanting to replicate your analysis on their own computer. If your code is not well organized, you will lose hours (or days) just trying to understand what you did.

Think of this guide as an investment in your **future self**:

- **Reproducibility** — anyone can repeat your analysis and obtain the same results
- **Transparency** — every step is documented in code
- **Portability** — your project works on any computer
- **Collaboration** — others can understand and contribute to your work

::: {.callout-tip}
## Open Science
A well-organized project can be uploaded as-is to [**OSF**](https://osf.io/) (Open Science Framework), Zenodo, or a GitHub repository. The folder structure, the README, commented scripts, and data in `raw_data/` together form a complete package that meets open science standards.
:::

# Project organization

## One project = one folder

Every study, article, or project should have its own folder with a consistent structure:

```
my_project/
├── my_project.Rproj      <-- RStudio project (sets the working directory)
├── README.txt            <-- Project description, variables, instructions
├── data/
│   ├── raw_data/         <-- Original data (NEVER modify)
│   └── processed_data/   <-- Cleaned/transformed data (generated by scripts)
├── scripts/              <-- Numbered R scripts (01_preprocessing.R, 02_analysis.R...)
├── output/               <-- Tables, figures, and exported results
├── docs/                 <-- Additional documentation (protocols, pre-registrations, manuals)
└── archive/              <-- Old versions of scripts or data (just in case)
```

**What you edit vs. what is generated automatically:** you edit `README.txt`, the `scripts/`, and the documentation in `docs/` directly. The contents of `data/processed_data/`, `output/` (and `archive/` when you move things there) are generated by running your scripts. The data in `data/raw_data/` is **read-only** — never modified.

## Use `.Rproj`

- Create an RStudio project: **File > New Project > Existing Directory**
- The `.Rproj` automatically sets the **working directory**
- Enables **relative paths** (portable across computers)
- **Never** open a script directly — always open the `.Rproj`

```r
# BAD: absolute path (only works on your computer)
read_csv("/Users/Filip/Desktop/my_project/data/raw_data/data.csv")

# GOOD: relative path (works on any computer)
read_csv("data/raw_data/data.csv")
```

## Don't touch raw data

Files in `data/raw_data/` are **read-only**. If you need to clean or transform data, save the result in `data/processed_data/`.

This way you can always return to the starting point.

# Script structure

## Header

Every script should start with:

```r
# ============================================================
# Script title
# Project: project name
# Author: your name
# Date: 2026-04-16
# ============================================================
```

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
## Anonymized peer review?

If you are going to share the script in an **anonymized peer review**, **remove the author's name and any identifying information** before submitting it (along with any mention of affiliation, email, acknowledgments, OSF IDs linked to your account, etc.).

A good habit is to keep **two versions** of the header:

- **Working version** with full authorship (your local copy)
- **Anonymized version** for the submitted manuscript: `# Author: [anonymized for review]`

After acceptance, you restore full authorship in the final published version.
:::


## Install packages only once

```r
# run ONCE, then comment out
# install.packages("tidyverse")
# install.packages("afex")
# install.packages("emmeans")
```

Never leave `install.packages()` uncommented in a shared script — it would run every time someone executes the script.

## Load packages at the top

Load **all** packages at the start of the script, never in the middle:

```r
# --- packages ---
library(tidyverse)
library(afex)
library(emmeans)
library(effectsize)
library(report)
```

For packages you only use once, use `package::function()`:

```r
# no need to load the whole package
sjPlot::tab_corr(data)
performance::check_model(model)
```

## Bonus: install and load in a single step

If you want your script to **self-manage** — automatically installing any packages that are missing on whoever's machine runs it — you can use `if (!require())`. This is useful for scripts you share and want to "just work".

For a single package:

```r
if (!require("tidyverse")) install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)
```

For several packages at once, you can use `c()` with a loop that walks over the vector:

```r
# packages your script needs
packages <- c("tidyverse", "afex", "emmeans", "effectsize", "report")

# install missing ones and load all
for (pkg in packages) {
  if (!require(pkg, character.only = TRUE)) {
    install.packages(pkg)
    library(pkg, character.only = TRUE)
  }
}
```

The `character.only = TRUE` argument is needed because `library()` and `require()` expect the package name **without quotes** by default. With `character.only = TRUE` we tell them: "the name is passed as a string variable".

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Alternative: `pacman`

The `pacman` package simplifies this pattern. `p_load()` loads packages and installs the missing ones automatically:

```r
# install.packages("pacman")  # only the first time
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, afex, emmeans, effectsize, report)
```

One single line, no `character.only = TRUE`, no loop. Recommended if you already have `pacman` installed.
:::

## Global options

```r
# --- options ---
options(scipen = 999)    # no scientific notation
options(width  = 120)    # console width (avoids truncating tables)
```

## Load data

```r
# --- load data ---
data_trial    <- read_csv("data/raw_data/data_clinical_trial.csv")
data_followup <- read_csv("data/raw_data/data_clinical_trial_followup.csv")
sites         <- read_csv("data/raw_data/data_sites.csv")
```

## sessionInfo() at the end

Include `sessionInfo()` at the end of every script. It records the R version, loaded packages, and operating system — essential for reproducibility.

```r
# --- at the end of the script ---
sessionInfo()
```

# Code conventions

The conventions below are based on the [**tidyverse style guide**](https://style.tidyverse.org/) (Hadley Wickham), the de facto standard in the R community. If you have any style question not covered in this guide, that's the first place to check.

## Variable names: `snake_case`

```r
# GOOD: snake_case (words separated by underscore)
anxiety_pre
mean_by_group
processed_data

# BAD: other styles
anxietyPre         # camelCase
MeanByGroup        # PascalCase
anxiety.pre        # dots (confusing with S3 methods)
anxiety-pre        # dashes (not a valid R name)
```

## Descriptive names

```r
# GOOD: names that explain what the object contains
processed_data <- data_trial |> ...
summary_by_group <- data_trial |> group_by(group) |> ...
simple_model   <- lm(anxiety_post ~ n_sessions, data = cbt)

# BAD: cryptic names
d  <- data_trial |> ...
x  <- data_trial |> group_by(group) |> ...
m1 <- lm(anxiety_post ~ n_sessions, data = cbt)
```

## Use English in code

Write your code in English: names of objects, variables, columns, functions, and files. Comments can be in any language you prefer.

```r
# GOOD: code in english, comments in your own language
data_trial <- read_csv("data/raw_data/clinical_trial.csv")

# keep only patients in the CBT group
cbt_group <- data_trial |>
  filter(group == "CBT")

mean_anxiety_post <- cbt_group |>
  summarise(mean = mean(anxiety_post))


# BAD: accented characters and mixed languages in code
ensayo_clínico <- read_csv("datos/datos_brutos/ensayo_clínico.csv")

grupo_TCC <- ensayo_clínico |>
  filter(grupo == "TCC")

media_ansiedad_después <- grupo_TCC |>
  summarise(media = mean(ansiedad_después))
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why English?
- **English is the universal language of science**: if you share code with a reviewer, a foreign collaborator, or upload it to GitHub, anyone can understand it.
- **No problematic characters**: English has no accents or `ñ`, so you avoid encoding issues.
:::

## Assign results to objects

Always save the result of a pipeline to an object with a descriptive name:

```r
# GOOD: stored and reusable
result <- data_trial |>
  filter(group == "CBT") |>
  summarise(mean = mean(anxiety_post))
result

# BAD: result is printed to the console and lost
data_trial |>
  filter(group == "CBT") |>
  summarise(mean = mean(anxiety_post))
```

## Comment your code

Comments should explain the **why**, not the **what**:

```r
# GOOD: explains the reason
# exclude control because they did not receive treatment
treatment_data <- data_trial |>
  filter(group != "control")

# BAD: repeats what the code already says
# filter data
treatment_data <- data_trial |>
  filter(group != "control")
```

## Spacing and readability

```r
# GOOD: spaces around operators and after commas
mean_value <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
data |> filter(age > 40, group == "CBT")

# BAD: no spaces
mean_value<-mean(x,na.rm=TRUE)
data|>filter(age>40,group=="CBT")
```

## Named arguments

In functions with many arguments, use explicit names:

```r
# GOOD: clear what each argument does
cor.test(x = data$anxiety_post,
         y = data$wellbeing_post,
         method = "pearson")

# LESS CLEAR: depends on argument order
cor.test(data$anxiety_post, data$wellbeing_post, "pearson")
```

## Native pipe `|>`

Use the native R pipe (`|>`) instead of the magrittr pipe (`%>%`):

```r
# GOOD: native pipe (R 4.1+)
data_trial |>
  filter(group == "CBT") |>
  summarise(mean = mean(anxiety_post))

# AVOID: magrittr pipe (legacy)
data_trial %>%
  filter(group == "CBT") %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(anxiety_post))
```

To enable the `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + M` shortcut:
**Tools > Global Options > Code > Use native pipe operator**

## `select()` across multiple lines

When selecting many columns, put each one on its own line:

```r
# GOOD: readable
data <- data_trial |>
  select(patient_id,
         group,
         anxiety_pre,
         anxiety_post,
         depression_pre)

# LESS READABLE: everything on one line
data <- data_trial |> select(patient_id, group, anxiety_pre, anxiety_post, depression_pre)
```

# Starting with a clean environment

When you work in R for hours, the *environment* (top-right panel in RStudio) accumulates objects: old data frames, test variables, half-finished models. **Your code may be working by accident, relying on objects that are no longer in your script.**

Your script should be able to run **from scratch, in a clean R session, top to bottom, without errors**. If it needs objects created "by hand" in the console, it is broken.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Disable "save workspace on exit"

By default, RStudio saves the environment between sessions (the `.RData` file). This perpetuates the problem: you open RStudio the next day and the old objects are still there.

**Disable it**: *Tools → Global Options → General → Workspace*:

- **Restore .RData into workspace at startup**: *No*
- **Save workspace to .RData on exit**: *Never*

When you restart the R session, you always start from zero. Your script is the **single source of truth** for your analysis.
:::

## Restart the R session

The robust way to start with a clean session is to **restart the R session** completely:

- **RStudio shortcut**: `Ctrl + Shift + F10` (Windows/Linux) or `Cmd + Shift + F10` (Mac)
- **Menu**: **Session → Restart R**
- **Effect**: R returns to the state of a freshly opened session — no objects, no packages, no changed options

After restarting, run your script from top to bottom. If it works, your analysis is reproducible. If it fails, you've found exactly the line that was missing.


## `rm(list = ls())` — not enough

The `rm(list = ls())` command **removes objects** from the environment, but leaves loaded:

- The **packages** (`library(tidyverse)` stays active)
- Changes to **global options** (`options(scipen = 999)`)
- Open **graphics devices**
- Manually modified functions

::: {.callout-warning}
## Avoid `rm(list = ls())` in shared scripts

If you put that line at the top of a script and someone (a collaborator, your future self) runs your code, **you silently wipe out all the objects in their environment**. They may lose important work.

The script describes **the analysis**. A clean environment is **the responsibility of whoever runs the script**, before hitting Run.
:::

## What about the broom icon?

The broom icon in the *Environment* pane **only removes objects** — equivalent to `rm(list = ls())`. It does not restart packages or options. Use it for quick cleanup during an exploratory session, but **for real reproducibility, restart the R session**.


# Reproducible documents with Quarto / R Markdown

A **Quarto** (`.qmd`) or **R Markdown** (`.Rmd`) file combines in a single document:

- **Text** in Markdown (prose, headings, tables, references)
- **R code chunks** that are executed when rendering
- **Results** from that code: tables, plots, model output

When you render the document, R runs all chunks in order and produces an HTML, PDF, or Word file with text + code + results integrated. It is the standard way to produce **reproducible analyses** in R.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## `.qmd` vs `.Rmd`: use Quarto

**R Markdown** (`.Rmd`) was the standard for years, but it has been superseded by **Quarto** (`.qmd`), its **modern successor** developed by the same team (Posit, formerly RStudio).

- **Quarto** supports more languages (R, Python, Julia, Observable), has better documentation, and is where all new development happens.
- **R Markdown** still works, but is no longer actively developed.

**For new projects, use `.qmd`**. If you inherit an old `.Rmd`, it's usually fine to keep it — the syntax is nearly identical, so transitioning is trivial if needed.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Typical workflow: `.R` for preprocessing, `.qmd` for analysis

Not everything needs to be a `.qmd`. A division that works well in practice:

- **`.R` scripts** (`scripts/01_preprocessing.R`) — for the heavy lifting: load data, clean, merge, save a `preprocessed_data.csv`. Run rarely, don't need formatted output.
- **`.qmd`** for the **final analysis** (`scripts/02_analysis.qmd`) — there you load the `preprocessed_data.csv` and run descriptives, models, plots, interpretation. Renderable, shareable, with prose and results integrated.

This keeps the raw-data handling **separate** from the reproducible analysis — and you can share the rendered HTML of the `.qmd` without dragging all the preprocessing along.
:::

## How to render in RStudio

When you open a `.qmd` or `.Rmd` file in RStudio, a button appears in the toolbar:

- **`.Rmd`** → **Knit** button with the blue **ball-of-yarn** icon
- **`.qmd`** → **Render** button with a blue arrow

Clicking it (or pressing `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K`), RStudio:

1. Runs all chunks in the document, top to bottom, in a clean session.
2. Captures the results: tables, plots, console output.
3. Generates an HTML (or PDF, or Word) file with everything combined.

## Basic structure

A `.qmd` has three parts:

- **YAML** (header with metadata: title, author, output format, global options)
- **Markdown text** (prose + `#` headings, lists, tables, links)
- **R chunks** between three backticks and `{r}`, each containing a block of code

::: {.callout-note}
## Self-contained HTML

In the YAML, add **`embed-resources: true`** (Quarto) or **`self-contained: true`** (R Markdown): that way the HTML is packaged into **a single file**, without an additional folder that can get lost when you email it or upload it to OSF.

Ready-to-use templates in `extra/analisis_completo_dia4.qmd` and `extra/analisis_completo_dia4_minimal.qmd` of this course ([see on GitHub](https://github.com/CIMCYC/curso-R-v2026/tree/master/extra)).
:::

## A useful application: sharing results when you cannot share the data

Sometimes you work with data you **cannot publish**: clinical data, data from minors, data protected by ethics agreements or institutional arrangements. In those situations, the classical "upload the data and the script to OSF or GitHub" doesn't work.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The alternative: share the rendered document

Render your analysis as `.qmd` or `.Rmd` and share **the resulting HTML**. That file includes:

- Your **code** (visible and copyable)
- The **results** of running it on your real data
- Your **interpretations**

The reviewer sees **what your analysis did**, without you sharing the original data. You keep confidentiality and reproducibility at the same time.
:::


# Best practices checklist

A summary list to review before saving, sharing, or publishing your analysis. If you can check all the boxes: open the `.Rproj`, restart the R session, run your script from the beginning, and you are ready to share.

## Project organization

- [ ] The whole analysis lives in **one folder** with its own `.Rproj`
- [ ] There is a `README.txt` with project description, variables, and instructions
- [ ] Original data in `data/raw_data/`, **never modified**
- [ ] Cleaned data in `data/processed_data/` (generated by scripts)
- [ ] Scripts numbered by execution order (`01_preprocessing.R`, `02_analysis.qmd`...)
- [ ] All paths in code are **relative**, not absolute

## Scripts

- [ ] Header with title, author, date, project
- [ ] `install.packages()` commented out (or wrapped in `if (!require())`)
- [ ] All `library()` calls at the top of the script
- [ ] `options(scipen = 999, width = 120)` at the top (if applicable)
- [ ] All data loaded at the top
- [ ] `sessionInfo()` at the end of the script

## Code style

- [ ] Object and variable names in `snake_case` **and in English**
- [ ] Descriptive names, not cryptic (`simple_model`, not `m1`)
- [ ] No accents or `ñ` in code (comments can have them)
- [ ] Named arguments in complex function calls (`cor.test(x = ..., y = ..., method = ...)`)
- [ ] Native pipe `|>` (not `%>%`)
- [ ] Pipeline results assigned to named objects
- [ ] Comments explain the **why**, not the **what**

## Reproducibility

- [ ] The script runs **from scratch** after `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + F10` (restart R session)
- [ ] No `rm(list = ls())` at the top of the script
- [ ] "Save workspace on exit" disabled in RStudio Global Options
- [ ] If using Quarto/RMarkdown: `embed-resources: true` in YAML for self-contained HTML
- [ ] If you cannot share the data: share the rendered `.html` with visible results

## Before publishing (OSF, Zenodo, GitHub)

- [ ] Personal or identifiable data **not** in the public repo
- [ ] `README.txt` updated with the latest file versions
- [ ] The rendered HTML runs without errors in a clean session
- [ ] Clear license (MIT, CC-BY, etc.) if the project is public
